Intravenous opioids administered in active labor cross the placenta rapidly and
can depress the neonatal respiratory drive
delivery. FDA labeling across multiple opioids (e.g.
sufentanil) explicitly warns of neonatal respiratory depression during labor and
recommends immediate availability of naloxone and neonatal monitoring.[1][2][3]
Reviews and consensus statements note that systemic opioids offer limited
mitigation of labor stress but are associated with fetal–neonatal depression;
respiratory depression is the clinically significant neonatal risk that necessitates
preparedness for resuscitation and reversal.[4][5][6][7] Small trials of IV fentanyl
during labor report generally normal Apgar and cord gases with careful dosing
and timing
column2: yet acknowledge the need for neonatal observation and readiness for

emphasizes monitoring for opioid-related neonatal effects when exposure
occurs near delivery.[9]
